# Transcrição: 06-Surprise

- **Fonte:** 06-Surprise
- **Duração:** 11:42
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

All right, buckle in. This is important. The most delightful thing we have to offer our audiences as storytellers is surprise. There is really nothing better, and there's nothing harder to achieve than surprise. I see it spoiled on a daily basis. The reason I chose to tell you the Maddie the Dog story is because that story is predicated on a surprise. You have to be surprised at the end of the story that Maddie the Dog has made a reappearance, and then you have to be surprised to discover that the bride has left her husband for the best man. If you're not surprised about those things, that story doesn't work. When my friends tell the Maddie the Dog story, they always say it this way, tell the story about the time when you lived above the dog that was named after you. And I always say, I think you just did. You just told the story because they spoil the surprise. What else is there to say unless that moment for you is surprising? Also the story is not about that. My dumb friends think it's about a coincidence when really it's a life-changing moment for me, but they're too dumb to see that kind of thing. All of it, though, is predicated on surprise. There are strategies that we can use to protect, preserve, and enhance the surprise that takes place in our stories. Surprise is also critical to emotional response. The only real way we can generate an emotional response from our audiences is through surprise. If I had time, I could produce other emotional responses from you. If I wanted to make you feel bad, I could spend days saying terrible things to you, and eventually you would crack. But our stories aren't that long. We have to produce surprise so people can react emotionally. The beginning of that story, for example, is water on raisin bran. That is surprising. It is surprising and it's intended to produce a laugh, and then right after a laugh, sadness. Oh, water on raisin bran, that's how sad he is. That is intended to be surprising to produce an emotional response. The first is laughter, and I want to laugh in the beginning of a story. We'll talk about that later, but then simultaneously sadness. It's why I choose a bathroom without a shower curtain. Surprising, again, funny, but also tragic. I'm looking to create an emotional response. But the big surprise in that story, and the one that I want to talk about, is the fact that Maddie the dog will reappear at the end of the story, and you must not see it coming. You have to be utterly surprised that this is a story about that dog.

## [03:00 - 06:02]

The way we do it is through a few steps. First of all, and the most important, you have to identify where the surprises take place. Nobody does this and it makes me crazy. It's so simple. All you have to do is say to yourself, when I was surprised, my audience should also be surprised. That's it. Identifying the location of surprises is the first step to actually surprising someone. If you don't, you have no hope of surprising anyone. I identified in that story that when Maddie the dog appears, my audience must be surprised. They can't see it coming. Once you identify the location of a surprise, there are some things that you can do. In that story, as in most surprises, the trick is to inform your audience about something that they need to know in order for the surprise to pay off, but you can't let them know how important it is. You're essentially putting information into the mind of the audience, making sure they know it, but also making sure they forget it, that they know it, but they don't see its importance. How do we do that? In that story, I use several tricks. The first thing I do is I introduce Maddie the dog, not as a big deal in the story, but I assign Maddie the dog a reason to be in the story. My wife has told me that our marriage is boring, and because she has told me this, I must prove to her that it's not true. Maddie the dog is not there to lift up the story or to prepare for a surprise later on, but just to argue against what my wife has said. I've given it reason to be in the story, and because it has a reason, the audience doesn't anticipate it having another reason. So we have to attach these bits of information to the story in a meaningful way. I need to introduce the idea of Maddie the dog without the audience seeing its importance, so you give it a reason to be there. The other thing I do is I add it to a list of things. Maddie the dog in that story is part of a list of reasons why I'm not boring. I'm a wedding DJ. I entertain guests every weekend. I have parties and music. Also, a couple once named their dog after me because I'm the funnest person they know. When we put information in lists, it often gets forgotten, or at least its importance is undermined. When you put something in a list, it's just one of many rather than one of one. So when you have that bit of information that you need the audience to know, but you don't want them to realize how important it is, you put it in a list of other things and it

## [06:02 - 09:08]

gets lost in the list. The third strategy is you make people laugh at the thing they need to know because when people laugh at things, they always think they're not important, which is why I use that line that says, mind you, my partner's name is Benji. They could have named the dog after the guy who was named after the dog, but instead they named the dog after me. That line is specifically constructed to make you laugh. And as you laugh, the existence of Maddie the dog is forgotten because when you laugh at something, how could it ever be the most important piece of information I'm providing to you so that the story makes sense at the end? So those three strategies are used upfront. I give the bit of information required a reason to be in the story, an important, relevant, realistic reason. I include it in a list and I make it funny. So now you're primed for the surprise. Now you have everything you need to know so that you're primed for the surprise to happen. But then we get to the surprise and we need to do some other things too. One of the things I did to you, one of the things I do so often is I lied to you. I convinced you of one thing and then made it the other. When Maddie the dog came around the truck, I said, the groom was holding the leash. What I did to you was in your mind, I turned that person into the groom and your mind created a groom holding a leash. Once you were convinced it was the groom, I said, but wait a minute, that's not the groom, but I know this person too. I made you think something and then I made you unthink it and that is surprising. The same kind of surprise I had. In fact, I gave you exactly the same emotional response that I had, which was, and then I see the groom, but wait, that's not the groom, but I know him too. I lied to you and then I told you the truth. One after the other. I put an idea in your head and then I flipped it. It's a great way to sort of cement that surprise at the end, telling the audience what they expect and therefore they will believe and then upending that expectation by telling them the truth. Then I allowed the surprise to settle with the audience. Hopefully you put it together before I ever verbalized it, that the wife and the best man were now together. I'm hoping all of those things happened for you through the course of that story. There were also other little subtleties that I included in the story that you didn't notice probably. For example, it's important that you know I have a dog because at the end of the story there's a man in the world who doesn't have his dog anymore and I still do, which means I need to tell you I have a dog, but I don't really want you to be thinking about my dog.

## [09:08 - 11:42]

I don't want dogs to be an important part of the story. So how do I introduce the presence of my dog? I use my dog as one of the sources of the smell of curry. Rather than telling you I have a dog named Kaylee, she's a lasso opso, she's my best friend, I love her so much, all I say is my dog smells like curry. That is me saying I have a dog, now forget about it. It'll be important later on, but I don't want you thinking about it now. It's my understanding that if I make a big deal out of my dog now and then Maddie the dog comes along, they're going to start thinking dog the whole way through the story and I have to make sure I don't do that. A dog that smells like curry is kind of funny and the dog that smells like curry is in a list of other things that smell like curry. I undermine the importance of my dog by using those strategies so that at the end when I'm sitting at that cruddy kitchen table and I say I still have my best friend and I still have my dog. When I say I still have my dog, I want you to go, that's right, he has a dog and he still has his dog. I want that surprise to settle with you. All of those strategies help me to make stories surprising and remember surprise is the most delightful thing we have to offer an audience. If you've ever seen any of those movies where the surprise at the end is profound, the usual suspects, the sixth sense, Fight Club, seven, there's tons of them, you know at the end of the movie how amazing that feels to suddenly have all of that come together and you realize my gosh they were building the surprise the whole way. That's what you have to offer audiences. Use my simple strategies. Find the information that your audience needs to know. Those critical elements that will pay off later on and use those strategies. Attach the information to the story in another way, give it a reason for being there. Include it in a list so it's forgotten. Make it funny so it doesn't seem important. And then lie to your audience. Confirm their expectation before you upend it. If you do those things you'll have those kinds of stories, the kind of presentation, the kind of pitch that will genuinely surprise your audiences and they will never forget it. Surprise it's the most delightful thing you have to offer to an audience. If you can find it and make sure that you preserve, protect and enhance it.
